3A_Bibliographic.citation: Nagata, J. M., Chu, J., Ganson, K. T., Murray, S. B., Iyer, P., Gabriel, K. P., Garber, A. K., Bibbins-Domingo, K., & Baker, F. C. (2023). Contemporary screen time modalities and disruptive behavior disorders in children: A prospective cohort study. Journal of Child Psychology and Psychiatry, 64(1), 125-135.
3A_Bibliographic.journal: Journal of Child Psychology and Psychiatry
3A_Bibliographic.notes: Published as HHS Public Access Author Manuscript

3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: We analyzed prospective data from the ABCD Study, a longitudinal study of brain development and adolescent health in 11,875 children recruited from 21 sites around the United States (U.S.)... Data analyzed are from the ABCD 3.0 release for the baseline (2016-2018, 9-10-years-old) and one-year follow-up (2017-2019, 10-11-years-old) assessments.

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Screen time (total and by modality: TV/movies, videos, video games, texting, video chat, social media)
3C_Exposure.modalities: total screen time, TV shows/movies, videos (YouTube), video games, texting, video chat, social media
3C_Exposure.operationalization: Continuous (weighted average hours/day) and categorical (binary >2 hours, and multiple categories: <2 hours, 2-4 hours, 4+ hours)
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: Screen time was determined based on child self-report using the ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey. Participants answered questions about typical hours per day spent on six different screen modalities (viewing/streaming TV shows or movies, watching/streaming videos [e.g. YouTube], playing videogames, texting, video chatting [e.g. Skype, Facetime], and social media [e.g. Facebook, Instagram, Twitter]) separately for weekdays and weekend days... The weighted average was calculated as:[(weekday average x 5) + (weekend average x 2)]/7

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder at 1-year follow-up
3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: Kiddie Schedule for Affective Disorders and Schizophrenia (KSADS-5), parent-reported
3D_Outcome.base_rate_if_binary: Conduct disorder: 1.9%; Oppositional defiant disorder: 6.3% at one-year follow-up
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: The ABCD Study utilized the Kiddie Schedule for Affective Disorders and Schizophrenia (KSADS-5), a computerized tool for categorizing child and adolescent mental health concerns based on the DSM-5, for the assessment of conduct and oppositional defiant disorders... Parents/caregivers completed all the modules of the KSADS-5 on behalf of their child.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Modified Poisson regression with robust standard errors (prevalence ratios)
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: age, sex, race/ethnicity, household income, highest parent education, parent marital status, baseline conduct disorder symptoms, baseline oppositional defiant disorder symptoms, baseline major depressive disorder, family history of psychopathology
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: Modified Poisson regression analyses using robust standard errors were conducted to calculate prevalence ratios estimating associations between baseline screen time (exposure variable) and conduct or oppositional defiant disorder at one-year follow-up (outcome variables), adjusting for confounders... Screen time was examined as a continuous variable and as a categorical variable with previously described thresholds... Propensity weights were applied to yield representative estimates based on the American Community Survey from the US Census.

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Prevalence ratio per hour/day of screen time
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: total screen time (PR 1.07, 95% CI 1.03-1.11) per day at baseline was prospectively associated with higher prevalence of conduct disorder

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Prevalence ratio per hour/day of social media
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: each hour of social media (prevalence ratio [PR] 1.62, 95% CI 1.39-1.87)

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Prevalence ratio per hour/day of screen time
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: total screen time (PR 1.05, 95% CI 1.03-1.08) was prospectively associated with higher prevalence of oppositional defiant disorder
each hour of video chat (prevalence ratio [PR] 1.21, 95% CI 1.06-1.37)

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Total screen time >4 hours/day vs <2 hours/day
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: exposure to >4 hours of total screen time per day was associated with a 69% higher prevalence of conduct disorder

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Total screen time >4 hours/day vs <2 hours/day
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: a 46% higher prevalence of oppositional defiant disorder

3G_Reporting_Quality.small_es_verbatim: The effect sizes for total screen time were relatively small as each additional hour of total screen time was associated with a 7% higher prevalence of conduct disorder and a 5% higher prevalence of conduct disorder... Although the effect sizes for screen time were small relative to other factors, such as baseline symptoms of conduct or oppositional defiant disorder or family history (i.e., genetic risk)... Despite smaller effect sizes, screen use is an important exposure because it can be modifiable through intervention
3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: Paper acknowledges small effect sizes for total screen time but then argues screen time matters because it is modifiable. Proceeds to issue clinical recommendations despite small effects.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: Parents should develop family media use plans and implement parental controls, as appropriate. Health care providers should consider assessing for behavioral problems among children with high screen use and advise about potential risks associated with excessive screen time.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.population_override_verbatim: Effect sizes are reported for each hour of screen exposure per day; therefore, total effects are greater with more hours per day of screen exposure. In addition, cumulative exposure to screen time over several years may yield stronger associations.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.modifiable_factor_verbatim: Despite smaller effect sizes, screen use is an important exposure because it can be modifiable through intervention, whereas the ability to intervene on genetics and baseline symptoms may be more limited.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.per_unit_verbatim: Each hour of total screen time per day was associated with a 7% higher prevalence of conduct disorder and a 5% higher prevalence of conduct disorder.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion acknowledges that effect sizes for total screen time are small but frames them as meaningful through per-unit accumulation arguments and modifiable-factor framing. The paper emphasizes social media as the strongest correlate and issues extensive clinical and public health recommendations including family media use plans, parental controls, and clinician screening despite the modest magnitude of the primary associations.
